data.title: These companies are matching Trump Account contributions for employees
data.description:
A number of companies have pledged to match the federal government’s $1,000 deposits in the “Trump accounts” for the children of employees. 

The Treasury Department is contributing $1,000 to the accounts of children born between Jan. 1, 2025, through Dec. 31, 2028. Employers of the child or their parent can contribute up to $2,500 per year to the accounts starting on July 5, according to the IRS. 

So far this week, Coinbase, JPMorgan Chase, Steak ‘n Shake and Turning Point USA have announced they will match that $1,000 contribution for the children of their employees. 

“JPMorganChase has demonstrated a long-term commitment to the financial health and well-being of all of our employees and their families around the world, including more than 190,000 here in the United States,” JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon said in a release. “By matching this contribution, we’re making it easier for them to start saving early, invest wisely, and plan for their family’s financial future.”

Turning Point USA CEO Erika Kirk wrote on social platform X Wednesday that the organization and its affiliate, Turning Point Action, will match Treasury’s contribution dollar-for-dollar “for every eligible employee’s newborn baby.”

“We’re proud to stand with @POTUS in supporting families and investing in the future of America,” she added.

During a Wednesday event touting the accounts, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that Broadcom, Intel, Comcast and Chipotle all pledged to match the $1,000 contribution earlier in the day. President Trump also said at the event that Continental Resources, Nvidia and Uber will also match the government’s deposit. 

The Hill has reached out to those companies to confirm their plans.

“Donations from employers are essential to bringing the President’s vision to life,” Bessent noted.

Other companies, such as BlackRock, BNY, Charles Schwab, Charter Communications, Robinhood and SoFi previously pledged to match the government’s contribution to the accounts of employee’s children. 

Employer deposits, which are pre-tax, count against the $5,000 annual cap on individual contributions to the accounts. Family members, friends and other adults can contribute starting in July. 

Charitable organizations and state government may also make contributions, which do not count against the annual limit. 

Last month, billionaire Michael Dell and his wife, Susan, pledged to donate $6.25 billion to the accounts of 25 million children, amounting to $250 per child. 

Dell said at Wednesday’s event that their donations are targeted to zip codes where the median income is less than $150,000.

Trump also praised singer Nicki Minaj, who attended the summit, for pledging to contribute “hundreds of the thousands of dollars” to the accounts of her fans’ children. The exact amount and qualification details are unclear.

data.title: Insane! CNN pundit still denies Charlie Kirk was killed by left-winger
data.description:
Why are left-wingers STILL denying that Charlie Kirk was killed by someone on their team? It’s been months, and I thought this vicious lie had been debunked numerous times. But here we go again. 

Tiffany Cross is a progressive pundit and podcast host. She previously had a show on MSNBC, and now makes occasional appearances on CNN. This week, she joined the panel for Abby Phillip’s show, alongside legal analyst Elie Honig, a moderate, and TV personality Kevin O’Leary, a libertarian-leaning conservative. It did not go well.  

Right off the bat, Cross repeated the provably false claim that Kirk was murdered by a right-wing extremist. And she conjured up this lie during a discussion of the recent attack on Rep. Ilhan Omar, who was assaulted by a deranged man who squirted an unknown liquid substance at her while she was speaking. Honig condemned this act of political violence — as everyone should — but then made the very fair observation that there is plenty of extremism on both sides. He went out of his way to assert that he did not necessarily think there were exactly equal amounts on both sides, only that conservative figures faced violence, too. But when he mentioned Kirk, Cross interrupted him to say that Kirk had been killed by a right-wing person.  

That is a lie.  

This notion that Tyler Robinson, Kirk’s alleged killer, had right-wing motivations has apparently remained appealing for leftists, ever since the progressive Substack writer Heather Cox Richardson helped to mainstream this idea in the immediate aftermath of the shocking murder. Nevertheless, it is absolutely false. While Robinson did indeed come from a Republican family, his own motivation for committing the assassination was that he disliked the things Kirk had said about transgender people. We know this because Robinson confessed it in messages to his romantic partner, a transgender individual, writing that he felt compelled to take action to stop Kirk from spreading hate against the transgender community. 

This is not really open to debate. Robinson is not a right-wing extremist, and his stated reasons for murdering Kirk were characteristic of left-wing thinking. 

Perhaps you can forgive a commentator for getting this wrong if it’s before all the facts come in, but that at this point — months after Kirk’s death — Cross is just repeating something that is completely debunked. 

She also argued with O’Leary about whether new recruits who have joined Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) are members of the Proud Boys, a far-right militia group. Cross asserted that she knew for a fact that Proud Boys had joined ICE, and cited as evidence the presence of what she described as “white supremacist” tattoos on the necks of some ICE officers. 

Now, there may be former Proud Boys who are members of law enforcement. And there might be members of law enforcement who have white nationalist tattoos. It’s true that one ICE agent attracted attention for having a Norse tattoo that some on the far-right claim as their own. I would caution about reading too much into this, however. Tattoos mean different things to different people. For 20 years, Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, had a tattoo plausibly associated with Nazi iconography on his chest. He claims he didn’t know that that’s what a totenkopf is. 

But Cross suggested that there were so many Proud Boys joining ICE that it was having an effect on the former group’s organizing. “There’s a reason why we have not seen a resurgence of the Proud Boys,” she said, “and that is because I believe a lot of them are likely made ICE officers.” 

That is NOT the reason we’ve seen less from the Proud Boys. We’ve seen less from the Proud Boys because they were sued, and many of them were in jail following January 6th. Enrique Tarrio, former chairman of the Proud Boys, was imprisoned for two years after being convicted of having organized the riot at the U.S. Capitol. Tarrio was also revealed to have been an FBI informant in 2012 and 2014.  

So in that sense, yeah, there have definitely been Proud Boys working for the federal government. But somehow, I don’t think that’s what Cross meant. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Noem silent during Trump Cabinet meeting
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem did not speak during President Trump’s Cabinet meeting on Thursday amid the administration’s shift in messaging on federal immigration operations in Minnesota. 

Noem was not the only Cabinet secretary to sit silently and not be called on during Thursday’s televised meeting, however the president has had each secretary speak in prior Cabinet gatherings. More attention was on the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) secretary for this meeting given scrutiny over the administration’s handling of two shootings in Minneapolis as well as negotiations playing out in Washington over DHS funding.

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the News Alerts newsletter

Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Housing and Urban Development Secretary Scott Turner, Energy Secretary Chris Wright, Environmental Protection Agency Administrator Lee Zeldin, Small Business Administrator Kelly Loeffler and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth all spoke during the meeting. Trump also gave Vice President Vance a chance to make brief remarks toward the end of the meeting. 

Trump also notably did not take any questions during the one hour and 19-minute meeting. The president frequently lets reporters ask questions after speeches and other public events. 

The meeting comes as the administration seeks to alter its tone and strategy on federal immigration operations in Minnesota following the shooting deaths of two Americans at the hands of immigration agents. The controversy did not come up once during the gathering. 

White House border czar Tom Homan, who Trump dispatched to Minnesota earlier in the week, gave a press conference from Minnesota earlier on Thursday in which he publicly acknowledged internal changes in the operations and vowed to “fix” the situation.

data.title: Durbin rips Vance over Duckworth insult: 'Low and beneath the dignity' of a VP
data.description:
Sen. Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) criticized Vice President Vance Thursday morning, calling comments he made about the other senator from Illinois, Tammy Duckworth (D), “low.”

“I have learned over many years in office that politics ain’t beanbag. But JD Vance’s attack and insult of my colleague, Tammy Duckworth, is low and beneath the dignity of a Vice President,” Durbin said in a post on the social platform X.

“Senator Duckworth has never backed away from a fight, even with a Vice President. She has fought for and won a seat in the Senate where she fights for veterans, those with disabilities, and the people of our state,” Durbin added, lauding his fellow Prairie State senator. 

“J.D. Vance should focus on repairing the chaos his President has created, not on a Senator who has earned the respect of the Senate for her courage and effectiveness.”

On Wednesday, Duckworth slammed Vance after the vice president bashed her line of questioning at a Senate hearing. He compared the Illinois Democrat to the fictional character Forrest Gump, who was portrayed as having a physical and intellectual disability.

“Forrest Gump ran toward danger in Vietnam. Your boss ran to his podiatrist crying bone spurs,” Duckworth wrote on X, noting President Trump’s medical draft deferments.

“Petty insults at the expense of people with disabilities won’t change the fact that you’re risking troops’ lives to boost Chevron’s stock price,” she added. “It’s my job to hold you accountable.”

Vance had posted on X earlier that “[w]atching Tammy Duckworth obsessively interrupt Marco Rubio during this hearing is like watching Forest Gump argue with Isaac Newton.”

The Hill has reached out to Vance’s office for comment.

data.title: Cortez Masto says she won't break with Democrats in latest shutdown fight
data.description:
Sen. Catherine Cortez Masto (D-Nev.) on Thursday said she would not break with the Democratic party if a new shutdown fight ensued due to significant pushback against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

Last year, Cortez Masto joined seven other party members to author a continuing resolution bucking Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer’s (D-N.Y.) protest over the decision not to extend health care subsidies. 

“There’s a difference now,” Cortez Masto told CNN’s Manu Raju.

“First of all I don’t support shutdowns, never have. The only one that we’re questioning right now is Homeland Security and rightfully so based on what we have seen the excessive force and demand for accountability for these officers. So, there has to be a call-out,” she added.

Cortez Masto mentioned that even some Republicans have agreed to hold off on funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) after two U.S. citizens, Alex Pretti and Renee Good, were shot and killed by immigration authorities in Minneapolis earlier this month. 

“There has to be a demand for transparency. We have been talking to Republicans who seem to understand the gravity of the situation societally and I’m confident we can land it,” the Nevada Democrat said.

“So the way this would ultimately work would be that if a deal is in fact reached it would fund most of the federal government up until the end of September but then this Homeland Security Department would be funded under a temporary spending bill and during that temporary spending about that short time frame that would give them time to negotiate new policies on ICE that Democrats are demanding to be part of the bill and signed into law,” she continued. 

Cortez Masto said the plan would help “avert” the current public crisis with ICE while noting there hasn’t been an exact agreement about how ICE’s policies should be changed. A group of bipartisan lawmakers have called on Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign following the criticism over her handling the aftermath of the two Americans’ deaths. 

While Trump has so far refused to fire Noem, the Nevada senator says the administration has signaled a willingness to adjust immigration enforcement operations. The president sent border czar Tom Homan to Minneapolis to quell outrage and protests while handling questions from the media regarding the investigations into the deaths of Good and Pretti. 

Trump has also personally spoken with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) to increase state and federal coordination. Both Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) have said the conversations with Homan were productive, though tensions remain high.

Cortez Masto told CNN, “the shifting nature of this and the decision by the White House to both negotiate and move in the Democrats direction is a sign of where they view the politics are after getting significant blowback across the country and the aftermath of Minneapolis.”

data.title: Cruz calls for arming of Iranian protesters
data.description:
Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) on Tuesday called for the U.S. to arm Iranian protesters amid the Iranian government’s crackdown in the country. 

“We should be arming the protesters in Iran. NOW,” Cruz wrote Tuesday in a post on the social platform X.  

“For the Iranian people to overthrow the Ayatollah—a tyrant who routinely chants ‘death to America’—would make America much, much safer,” Cruz’s post continued.

The Trump administration has moved ships and military assets to the region, raising suggestions that a strike against Iran is possible. President Trump has previously warned Iran of potential strikes over its handling of antigovernment protests.

Cruz’s post was in response to a Jan. 26 post on X from the Tehran Bureau. It quoted a “trusted source in Tehran” who detailed brutal government actions against Iranian protesters and pleaded for those outside Iran to continue to help by holding protests in other countries.

Thousands of protesters have taken to the streets since Dec. 28 to protest the country’s worsening economic conditions and the rapidly decreasing Iranian currency.

In response, the government has imposed an internet blackout, shut down phone connections and killed more than 6,000 people, according to The Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA), an independent U.S.-based advocacy organization for human rights in Iran.

data.title: Americans are living longer: Why caregivers are headed for a crisis
data.description:
ATLANTA — By 2030, approximately 1 in 5 Americans will be over the age of 65, according to federal projections. Experts warn the United States is hugely unprepared. There is no comprehensive caregiving policy, no Medicare coverage for long-term care and not enough trained and affordable caregivers to meet the future wave. 

The result is a growing crisis for families — one that is already playing out in about 60 million households.

Neal Shah, CEO of CareYaya, is among those sounding the alarm. Shah is part of the so-called “sandwich generation,” a growing group of Americans simultaneously caring for their children and their aging parents. 

An aging population coupled with declining birth rates creates a smaller labor force that cannot keep pace with demand, Shah told “Raising America.” By 2030, the U.S. is expected to need millions more home care workers, even as the pool of available caregivers continues to shrink.

Shah points out that Americans are living longer than ever, and Medicare coverage does not include most long-term caregiving needs such as in-home assistance, memory care or extended custodial care. 

Families are left to shoulder the cost, often draining savings, retirement accounts or forfeiting college funds.

“Things have changed in the last 50 years thanks to advances in health care and medical technology. A lot of people are now able to live longer, right? If they’re able to live healthier independently, that’s a separate question. But they’re living physically longer.”

The CDC reports that U.S. life expectancy reached a record high of 79 years in 2024, a rebound from 2020 and evidence people are living longer. That longevity has created a ripple effect. Adult children are now expected to fill caregiving roles — often while working full-time and raising families of their own — without the policy support, financial assistance or infrastructure to make it sustainable. 

For many, turning a home into a multigenerational caregiving space simply isn’t possible. The financial burden can be staggering.

Shah said a memory care facility for a parent could cost anywhere from $120,000 to $200,00 a year. “You’re literally buying a new Porsche every year,” he said.

Beyond the financial strain, Shah noted a major cultural shift in who provides care. 

“A woman, traditionally, has had a lot of the stress,” he said. “Nowadays, the man is also experiencing stress just because of the way our society is,” because people started having fewer kids about a generation ago. 

He said about, “42 percent of people in this country that are caring for aging parents are actually male, which is like compared to 30 or 40, years ago, it would have been disproportionately 80 percent-plus would have been female. And it’s because a lot of times that’s the only child, or that’s the kid that’s living closest to the parents. So I think it’s actually reshaping a lot of family dynamics.”

Despite the scope of the problem, Shah argues solutions are possible if innovation fills the gaps that public policy isn’t. He points to new caregiving models that connect families directly with vetted caregivers, cutting out agency overhead that often reduces caregiver pay while increasing family costs. In Shah’s experience, when agencies take a share of caregiver wages, it leads to lower pay and higher turnover — worsening shortages.

“I think that’s a big problem, that the cost of care is too high, but the quality of care is low because the worker is getting less than half of what you’re paying,” he said. 

Shah said he wants CareYaya Health Technologies to become a health-tech platform designed to address those gaps. His goal is to connect families with health-focused student caregivers. The model aims to expand access to affordable, in-home care while building a pipeline of future health care workers so it’s a direct response to the national caregiver shortage.

“When you survey older adults and even middle-aged people, how do you want to die? Over 80 percent of them say, in my home, surrounded by family, when you observe the people that died last year, how did they die? Less than 20% of them, most of them died in bright frost or lights at the hospital, on a bunch of machinery. And I think that is a freaking tragedy, you know. So, we don’t do end of life care well,” he said. 

“Raising America” is a NewsNation podcast hosted by Elizabeth Prann.

data.title: Jeffries threatens quick Noem impeachment push, says Stephen Miller also 'needs to go'
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said Thursday that House Democrats are poised to launch impeachment proceedings against Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem if President Trump doesn’t remove her from power “immediately.”

“She’s got to go — we are dead serious,” Jeffries told reporters in the Capitol. “She needs to be put on ice permanently.” 

Jeffries did not put a date on the launch, but said he spoke with Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) on Wednesday to warn GOP leaders that the effort to topple Noem is imminent.

“If Kristi Noem is not fired immediately, impeachment proceedings will commence,” Jeffries said.

Jeffries also declared that Stephen Miller, Trump’s deputy chief of staff and a leading proponent of the administration’s deportation agenda, should also be removed.

“Stephen Miller is the architect of Donald Trump’s toxic immigration policies,” Jeffries said. “He’s a stone-cold liar. He’s a bigot, he’s a hateful individual. He’s a malignant, unelected interloper. 

“He needs to go as well.” 

Noem has come under heavy scrutiny in recent weeks over the Department of Homeland Security’s (DHS) deportation operations in Minneapolis, where two American citizens were fatally shot by federal officers this month as they protested Trump’s immigrant crackdown. 

The first incident, on Jan. 7, led to the death of Renee Good, a 37-year-old mother of three, who was killed in her car by a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer amid federal operations in a southern neighborhood of the city.

The most recent incident occurred last Saturday, when Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, was fatally shot by at least two Borer Patrol agents during a similar street protest in the same city. 

In both cases, Noem defended the federal law enforcers, characterizing both Good and Pretti as domestic terrorists who had threatened the lives of the officers. Miller delivered a similar message, calling Pretti “an assassin [who] tried to murder federal agents.” 

The administration’s narrative is contradicted by video footage of the shootings, and it’s infuriated the Democrats now calling for Noem’s impeachment. 

“She’s a disgrace, and on top of all that, she’s presided over the cold-blooded killings of American citizens,” Jeffries said Thursday.  

“She should have never been confirmed,” he continued. “Shame on every Republican in the Senate who put this wildly unqualified person into a position of significance at the Department of Homeland Security so she could unleash brutalization on everyday Americans, American citizens and law-abiding immigrant families.”

Although Democrats are the minority party in the House, they can use procedural tools to force a vote on an impeachment resolution. It’s unclear, however, if there are any House Republicans willing to cross the aisle and support such an explosive measure. 

Two Senate Republicans have called for Noem to step down or be fired.

A resolution impeaching Noem, sponsored by Rep. Robin Kelly (D-Ill.), currently has 156 co-sponsors, all of them Democrats. 

In 2024, House Republicans had impeached President Biden’s DHS secretary, Alejandro Mayorkas, over allegations that his border policies were threatening public safety. Even the most moderate Democrats opposed the measure, and they were joined by three Republicans.

data.title: Congressional budget process failures defy big fix
data.description:
You know something climactic is about to occur in Congress when even senior legislators begin to toss around sports metaphors like “a hail Mary pass” or “a quarterback sneak.”

Last week, 11-term Rep. Virginia Foxx (R-N.C.), chair of the House Rules Committee, at a meeting on granting special rules for six remaining appropriations bills, observed that the measures were the product of bipartisan and bicameral agreements.

“With this final tranche of appropriations bills coming before the committee,” she said, “the House stands on the one-yard line — we’re primed to complete consideration of full year [fiscal 2026] funding across all 12 bills.”

But even when poised to score from the one-yard line, things can go wrong. An interception or fumble can turn the whole game around.

That’s what almost happened last week when House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) announced that his Democratic colleagues would oppose the Homeland Security funding bill, which includes funding for ICE. Jeffries said changes must be made to rein in immigration enforcement and the violent tactics that have resulted in two recent killings of U.S. citizens in Minneapolis. The Senate was not far behind, as Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) threatened to take the whole package of remaining bills down if the ICE component was either not stripped or not significantly altered.

The special House rule that made in order both the three-bill package for the departments of Defense, Labor-HHS, and Transportation, and the separate bill for Homeland Security, narrowly passed the House last Thursday, 214-213. The Homeland Security bill subsequently passed, 220 to 207, with seven Democrats breaking ranks. The three-bill package passed by a more comfortable vote of 341-88.

All four measures were bundled into a mini-bus bill by the rule along with two other appropriations bills previously passed by the House for financial services and the Department of State before being sent to the Senate where debate began Tuesday. The Senate outcome was too close to call at this writing, as even the Democratic dealmakers of the bipartisan-bicameral compromise withdrew their support unless tough new restrictions are imposed on ICE.

That approach did not sit well with Republican leaders, who thought the compromise deal had been sealed. If the votes are no longer there and further alterations in ICE’s authority and tactics are not struck, another government shutdown may be imminent at midnight tomorrow — the deadline imposed in last year’s compromise that ended the record-breaking 43-day government shutdown.

The delay in the government funding process is nothing new. According to “Vital Statistics on Congress,” in fiscal years 1977 through 2019, Congress enacted all 13 (now 12) regular appropriations bills only three times before the start of the fiscal year — all in the last century. It has enacted none of the regular bills before Oct. 1 on 15 occasions, 12 of those in this century.

To call the budget process broken is an understatement. Mega process reforms have a tendency to become top-heavy with complex demands and eventually fail. The big reforms of the 1970s in the wake of Vietnam, Watergate and the imperial presidency, whether on war powers, intelligence oversight, or budgetary transparency and fiscal discipline, have all come a cropper. I call it the Humpty Dumpty phenomenon: they are all propped atop a grand wall, built on high hopes, and eventually destined to suffer a great fall.

Can the budget process be fixed? Probably not. The history of congressional reform efforts is littered with the carcasses of overly ambitious efforts designed to address every imaginable defect. This year’s fiscal foibles at budgeting track the recent historical pattern of eroding discipline and lax enforcement as Congress becomes more bitterly partisan. Further process reforms are the last thing members are seeking. Instead, they are more intent on launching partisan messaging campaigns aimed at picking up more seats at the next election. The sports metaphors seem less farfetched as politics becomes a blood sport.

Ironically, it may take divided party government to bring Congress back to its intended purpose of making all laws necessary and proper to promote the general welfare. Divided government can lead to more gridlock, but it can also force members to recognize that their political fortunes depend on working together to find common ground, especially in times of national crises and flagging public trust in government.

There is no sports analogy for opposing teams joining forces to accommodate most fans in the stands most of the time. No flag can be thrown to incentivize sportsmanlike conduct.

Don Wolfensberger is a 28-year congressional staff veteran culminating as chief of staff for the House Rules Committee in 1995. He is author of, “Congress and the People: Deliberative Democracy on Trial” (2000), and, “Changing Cultures in Congress: From Fair Play to Power Plays” (2018).

data.title: 'National Shutdown' set for Friday: What to know
data.description:
Video above: Government funding deadline looms as Democrats oppose ICE actions

(NEXSTAR) – In response to recent U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) activity, as well as the deaths of four people shot by federal agents, activists are encouraging a “shutdown” on Friday.

The “National Shutdown” calls for supporters to stay home from work and school, and not to go shopping in an effort to “stop funding ICE.”

“The people of the Twin Cities have shown the way for the whole country — to stop ICE’s reign of terror, we need to SHUT IT DOWN. On Friday, January 30, join a nationwide day of no school, no work and no shopping,” organizers wrote online.

Last Friday, businesses across Minnesota shuttered in protest of ICE being in the Minneapolis area. A day later, Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care unit nurse, was fatally shot by federal agents.

Organizers of Friday’s National Shutdown also pointed to the shooting deaths of Renee Good, Silverio Villegas González, and Keith Porter Jr., saying “they were gunned down in broad daylight simply for exercising their First Amendment right to protest mass deportation.”

“It is time for us to all stand up together in a nationwide shutdown and say enough is enough!”

Several University of Minnesota-based organizations, including the Somali Student Association, the Ethiopian Student Association, the Black Student Union, the Liberian Student Association, and the Graduate Labor Union, have endorsed Friday’s National Shutdown.

The political organization 50501 (50 States, 50 Protests, 1 Day), which has been at the center of several national protests since President Trump took office, is also endorsing the event. (50501, too, is supporting “ICE Out of Everywhere” events, hosted by Action Network, which are scheduled for Saturday.)

The website for Friday’s National Shutdown lists hundreds of other supporting organizations across the U.S.

Actors Pedro Pascal and Hannah Einbinder, as well as rapper Macklemore are among those who have promoted the general strike in separate Instagram posts.

“No system built on violence has ever been undone by reform,” the latter wrote. “It ends when people stop sustaining it. This is that moment.”

As they were during Minnesota’s statewide strike, businesses are being encouraged to close on Friday in solidarity. Several establishments in Denver are doing just that, Nexstar’s KDVR reported Thursday.

“It has been increasingly difficult to watch what is unfolding in our country,” a representative for award-winning Denver restaurant Sắp Sửa said in a post announcing its one-day closure. “We have felt so helpless and alone and it’s abundantly clear that no one will come to save us, so it is our civic duty to unite as a community in support of the most vulnerable.”

New ‘No Kings’ protests scheduled

Earlier this week, organizers announced a third round of “No Kings” protests coming this spring, to oppose what they describe as authoritarianism under Trump.

Previous rallies have drawn millions of people, and organizers said they expect even greater numbers on March 28 in the wake of Trump’s immigration crackdown in Minneapolis.

“We expect this to be the largest protest in American history,” Ezra Levin, co-executive director of the nonprofit Indivisible, told The Associated Press ahead of Wednesday’s announcement. He predicted that as many as 9 million people will turn out.

“No Kings” protests, which are organized by a constellation of groups around the country, have been a focal point for outrage over Trump’s attempts to consolidate and expand his power.

ICE operations winding down in Minnesota, Maine

Meanwhile, Trump’s border czar, Tom Homan, said Thursday that the administration is open to a drawdown of agents in Minneapolis but only after he sees cooperation from state officials.

And in Maine, Sen. Susan Collins said that immigration officials have ceased their “enhanced operations” in the state after more than 100 arrests in an enforcement surge. She made the announcement after saying she had several direct communications with Noem.

“There are currently no ongoing or planned large-scale ICE operations here,” Collins said in a statement. “I have been urging Secretary Noem and others in the Administration to get ICE to reconsider its approach to immigration enforcement in the state.”

The Associated Press contributed to this report.
